Keywords: pre-mortem, failure analysis, risk identification, hidden risks, prospective hindsight, blind spots
Description: The group assumes the decision has already been made and failed catastrophically. Participants work backward to identify what went wrong.
When to Use: Before committing to a high-stakes decision; surfaces hidden risks and breaks optimism bias in groups
Steps: 1. Facilitator states the proposed decision clearly. 2. Ask the group to imagine it is 12 months later and the decision failed spectacularly. 3. Each person independently writes 3-5 reasons it failed (5 min silent). 4. Go around the room, each person shares one reason per round. 5. Compile all failure modes on a shared board. 6. Vote on most likely and most severe. 7. For top risks, develop mitigation plans or reconsider decision.
Output: Prioritized list of failure modes with mitigation plans; revised risk assessment

Keywords: red team, adversarial, challenge, attack, critique, stress test, opposing view, counter-argument
Description: A dedicated sub-group is tasked with building the strongest possible case AGAINST the proposed decision, using all available evidence.
When to Use: When the group seems too aligned too quickly; provides adversarial testing of the preferred option
Steps: 1. Assign 2-3 people as the Red Team. 2. Red Team prepares the strongest case against the proposed decision (30-45 min). 3. Main group presents the case for the decision (15 min). 4. Red Team presents their counter-case (15 min). 5. Open discussion focusing on the strongest counter-arguments. 6. Decision-maker summarizes: which Red Team points change the decision or require mitigation?
Output: Counter-arguments ranked by strength; decision modifications; risk mitigations from adversarial analysis

Keywords: nominal group, silent generation, equal voice, democratic, structured brainstorm, round-robin, balanced input
Description: Structured process ensuring every participant contributes ideas independently before group discussion, preventing dominant voices from suppressing input.
When to Use: When dominant voices or hierarchy suppress diverse input; ensuring every person's perspective is captured
Steps: 1. State the decision question clearly. 2. Silent idea generation: each person writes their thoughts independently (10 min). 3. Round-robin sharing: each person shares one idea per round (no discussion yet). 4. Group discussion: clarify and explore each idea. 5. Independent ranking: each person privately ranks their top options. 6. Tally votes and discuss the results.
Output: Ranked list of options with vote counts; diverse perspectives captured; transparent prioritization

Keywords: structured debate, steel man, strongest argument, fair comparison, two options, rigorous, adversarial collaboration
Description: Two teams present the strongest possible case (steel man, not straw man) for opposing options, enabling rigorous comparison of the best version of each.
When to Use: When two strong options exist and the group needs rigorous comparison rather than superficial pros-cons
Steps: 1. Identify the two leading options. 2. Divide into two teams (ideally mixing genuine preferences). 3. Each team prepares the strongest case for their assigned option (20 min). 4. Team A presents (10 min), Team B asks clarifying questions (5 min). 5. Team B presents (10 min), Team A asks clarifying questions (5 min). 6. Open discussion. 7. Individual vote or decision-maker decides.
Output: Side-by-side comparison of strongest cases; balanced evaluation; decision with documented rationale

Keywords: dot voting, democratic prioritization, quick ranking, simple voting, group priority, rapid alignment
Description: Each participant allocates a fixed number of votes (dots) across options, producing a quick democratic ranking of preferences.
When to Use: Quick democratic ranking of criteria, options, or priorities when time is limited and approximate consensus is sufficient
Steps: 1. List all options/criteria on a visible board. 2. Give each participant N dots (typically 3-5, roughly 1/3 of total options). 3. Each person places dots on their priority items (can stack or spread). 4. Count dots per item. 5. Rank by total dots. 6. Discuss top-ranked items and any surprising results.
Output: Ranked list of priorities by group vote; visual heat map of preferences; quick consensus check

Keywords: anonymous, secret ballot, hidden input, honest opinion, hierarchy-free, safe, written input, candid
Description: Participants submit their real opinions or concerns anonymously before any group discussion, neutralizing hierarchy and political pressure.
When to Use: When hierarchy, politics, or social pressure bias open discussion; getting honest input from all levels
Steps: 1. Frame the decision question clearly. 2. Distribute anonymous submission method (digital form or paper). 3. Each person submits their honest assessment: preferred option and top concerns (10 min). 4. Collect and display all submissions without attribution. 5. Facilitator groups themes and highlights surprising inputs. 6. Discuss the anonymous themes openly. 7. Move to named discussion now that all perspectives are surfaced.
Output: Unfiltered perspectives from all participants; honest assessment of concerns; foundation for transparent discussion

Keywords: devil's advocate, critical challenge, appointed critic, question assumptions, constructive opposition, designated dissenter
Description: One person is formally assigned to challenge the preferred option, ensuring critical thinking even when the group converges on a choice.
When to Use: When the group has a preferred option that needs critical challenge before commitment; prevents premature consensus
Steps: 1. Identify the favored option. 2. Assign one person as devil's advocate (rotate this role across decisions). 3. Devil's advocate prepares the strongest counter-case (15 min). 4. Group presents the case for the favored option. 5. Devil's advocate challenges with counter-arguments, questions, and alternative interpretations. 6. Group addresses each challenge. 7. Document which challenges were resolved and which remain as risks.
Output: List of challenges to preferred option; unresolved risks documented; decision strengthened by stress testing

Keywords: workplan, alignment, execution, implementation, after decision, action items, owners, timeline, accountability
Description: After the decision is made, the group aligns on who does what by when — translating the decision into an executable workplan with clear accountability.
When to Use: After a major decision is made; aligning the team on execution plan to prevent decision-execution gap
Steps: 1. Restate the decision clearly. 2. Break implementation into work streams. 3. For each work stream: define deliverables, owner, timeline, resources needed. 4. Identify dependencies between streams. 5. Set check-in milestones. 6. Each owner confirms understanding and commitment. 7. Document and distribute the workplan.
Output: Detailed workplan with owners, timelines, milestones, and dependencies; shared commitment to execution
